content.heading: Array methods — useful tools built into every array

blocks.text: Arrays come with built-in methods that let you sort, copy, compare, and search them. These are used constantly in Simon Says — especially when checking if the player clicked the right squares in the right order.

blocks.desc: Sorts the items in the array in place. Numbers and strings sort differently — always check the result.
Returns a copy of the array without changing the original. Use this before sorting so the original stays intact.
Converts the whole array into a single string. [3,1,2].toString() gives '3,1,2'. Useful for comparing two arrays.
Returns the index of the first matching value, or -1 if not found.

lines: // Sort copies and compare as strings
console.log(a === b);  // true — same numbers, order doesn't matter

blocks.label: Always .slice() before .sort()
blocks.text: .sort() changes the original array. If you sort litCells directly, you lose the order the game chose. Always sort a copy: array.slice().sort()

exercises.title: Compare two sequences
tasks: Before running: predict whether each comparison below is true or false.
Run the code and check your predictions.
For any wrong ones — explain why.
exercises.hint: Remember: .slice() makes a copy, .sort() reorders, .toString() converts to a string for comparison.

exercises.title: Build the answer checker
tasks: Write a function called checkAnswer(lit, clicks) that returns true if the two arrays contain the same numbers (order doesn't matter), and false if they don't.
Test it with matching arrays, non-matching arrays, and arrays in different orders.
Log 'Correct!' or 'Wrong.' based on the result.
This is the exact function Simon Says uses to check the player's answer.
exercises.hint: Sort a copy of each array and compare as strings: lit.slice().sort().toString() === clicks.slice().sort().toString()

checks.label: checkAnswer returns true for matching arrays in different order
checkAnswer returns false for non-matching arrays

challenges.title: The sort is changing the original
challenges.description: This code should compare two arrays without changing them. But after comparing, litCells is in the wrong order. Find the bug and fix it.
challenges.hint: Sort should never touch the originals. What method makes a copy of an array?
challenges.explanation: .sort() sorts in place — it changes the original array. Use .slice() first to make a copy: litCells.slice().sort(). Now litCells stays as [5, 2, 8] and only the copy gets sorted.

challenges.title: indexOf is returning -1 but the value is in the array
challenges.description: The player clicked cell 3 but the game says it's not in the sequence. Why?
challenges.hint: indexOf checks type AND value. What type is playerClick? What type are the values in litCells?
challenges.explanation: litCells contains numbers [1, 3, 7] but playerClick is the string '3'. indexOf uses strict comparison — '3' !== 3 — so it returns -1. Fix: convert playerClick to a number with Number(playerClick) before the indexOf check.
